title: The Lodge of Forgotten Wives
author: C. M. Holloway
description: Lena thought Marcus's terrible Valentine's gifts were proof of his endearing cluelessness. But when he surprises her with a weekend at a remote mountain lodge, she discovers his mistakes were never mistakes at all — and the lodge has been waiting for her arrival for a very long time.

chapters.title: Chapter 1 — The History of Bad Gifts

content.text: Lena had learned to dread Valentine's Day. Not because she was unloved — Marcus loved her, she was certain of that in the way one is certain of gravity or the rising of the sun — but because his gifts, year after year, were catastrophes wrapped in good intentions. The first year, he gave her a cordless vacuum cleaner. She had laughed, thinking it was a joke, but his earnest, beaming face told her otherwise. 'You said the old one was heavy,' he explained, and she had kissed him and thanked him and stored the vacuum in the cupboard where she would never have to look at it. The second year, a slow cooker, because she had once mentioned in passing that stews were comforting. The third year, tickets to a football match — a sport she had never expressed any interest in — because he thought they should 'share his passions.' Each gift was a monument to his inability to truly see her, a portrait of a woman he had constructed in his mind that bore only a passing resemblance to the real Lena.

But the gifts were not the worst of it. The worst was the year he had leaned across the table at a candlelit restaurant and whispered, 'Lena, you've got a grey hair. Right there, at the temple. I thought you'd want to know.' She had cried in the bathroom, not because of the grey hair — she was forty-three, she had earned it — but because he had chosen that moment, that setting, to remind her that he was cataloguing her decline. She had forgiven him, of course. He was a good man. He brought her coffee every morning, left little notes on the bathroom mirror, held her when her mother died. The bad gifts were a quirk, she told herself. A blind spot. Every marriage had them.

This year, she had decided to take no chances. Operation Valentine, she called it. She left spa brochures on the kitchen table, opened the website of the local day resort on the shared computer, and mentioned to their daughter in a voice loud enough for Marcus to hear that all she really wanted was a day of pampering. She even circled a date on the calendar and wrote 'Maybe spa day?' in her neatest handwriting. When Marcus asked, casually, what she wanted this year, she looked him in the eye and said, 'I want to be surprised with something I'll actually love. Something that shows you know me.' He had nodded solemnly, and she had allowed herself to hope.

chapters.title: Chapter 2 — The Road to the Mountain

content.text: On the morning of February 14th, Marcus led her to the car with a hand over her eyes. 'No peeking,' he said, his voice trembling with excitement. She heard the crunch of gravel, the click of the boot opening, the thud of a suitcase. A suitcase. Her heart lifted. He had booked a trip. The spa, she thought. Finally, he had understood. They drove for hours, leaving the familiar suburbs behind, climbing into hills she had never seen. The road narrowed, the trees thickened, and the sky turned the colour of a bruise. 'Where are we going?' she asked, half-laughing. 'You'll see,' he said, and his smile was the smile of a man who had finally got it right.

They pulled through a pair of wrought-iron gates that hung open like broken jaws. The drive was long and winding, lined with dead rhododendrons, their leaves brown and curled. At the end, a stone lodge rose against the grey sky, its windows dark, its chimney smokeless. A sign, faded and weather-beaten, read: 'The Stillwater Lodge — A Retreat for the Weary.' Lena felt a prickle of unease. 'This isn't a spa,' she said slowly. Marcus parked the car and turned to her, his eyes glittering. 'It's better than a spa. It's a place where we can really connect. No distractions. Just us.' His hand closed over hers, cold and dry, and she felt the unease sharpen into something closer to fear.

Inside, the lodge was immaculate but empty. A fire crackled in the hearth; candles flickered on every surface; the air smelled of lavender and something else, something metallic and familiar that she could not quite place. A woman in a grey uniform appeared, her face smooth and blank as a doll's, and took their coats without speaking. 'Where are the other guests?' Lena asked. The woman smiled, a mechanical stretching of the lips, and said, 'There are no other guests. You are the only ones. Mr. Vance arranged a private stay.' Lena turned to Marcus, who was watching her with an expression she had never seen before: a kind of hungry, patient tenderness. 'Do you like it?' he asked. She nodded, not trusting her voice.

That evening, after a dinner of roast lamb that was perfectly cooked and perfectly tasteless, Lena excused herself to use the bathroom. She wandered the corridor, looking for the sign, and found herself in a wing of the lodge that was darker than the rest. The doors here were closed, each one with a small brass plate bearing a name: 'Catherine,' 'Eleanor,' 'Margot,' 'Beatrice.' Four doors. Four names. And then a fifth door, the nameplate empty, waiting. A cold dread pooled in her stomach. She tried the handle of the first door. It opened. Inside was a bedroom, frozen in time: a floral dress laid out on the bed, a hairbrush on the dresser, a pair of spectacles on the nightstand. On the pillow, a single grey hair. She backed away and opened the second door. Another bedroom, another dress. The third: a slow cooker, still plugged in, a faint warmth radiating from the pot. The fourth: a vacuum cleaner, its cord wrapped meticulously, its bag full. Lena stumbled back into the corridor, her heart hammering.

She found Marcus in the lodge's library, sitting in a leather chair, a glass of wine in his hand. 'You've seen the rooms,' he said. It was not a question. His voice was calm, conversational, as if they were discussing the weather. 'I built this lodge for you, Lena. For all of you. Each wife who tried to leave me, who complained about my gifts, who failed to appreciate the love I was offering. They're all here. Preserved. Perfect. Never ageing, never nagging, never asking for more than I was willing to give.' He stood and walked toward her, and she saw that his eyes were not glittering with excitement but with something else — a deep, flat emptiness that had been there all along, hidden beneath the coffee and the notes and the held hands. 'You wanted a surprise,' he said. 'This is it. You'll never have to worry about Valentine's Day again.'

Lena ran. She fled down the corridor, past the empty rooms, past the grey-uniformed woman who watched her with that same blank smile, past the dead rhododendrons and through the iron gates. The road was dark and cold and endless, and her breath came in ragged gasps. Behind her, the lodge glowed like a hearth in a fairy tale, warm and inviting and utterly monstrous. She did not stop running until she reached the nearest town, miles away, and even then she did not feel safe. Because she knew, with a sickening certainty, that Marcus would find her. He was patient. He collected the wives who tried to leave. And the empty nameplate was still waiting.

The police found the lodge exactly as Lena described it. The four rooms, the four sets of belongings, the four bodies buried in the garden beneath the dead rhododendrons. Each woman had been reported missing by a grieving husband named Marcus Vance. The police dug deeper and found that Marcus Vance was not one man but a series of identities, a shell company of a soul that had moved from state to state, marrying and disposing and moving on. In the lodge's basement, they found a fifth room, already prepared: a floral dress from the 1990s laid out on the bed, a hairbrush, a pair of spectacles. On the pillow, a single strand of Lena's hair, grey at the root. She had escaped, but the room was ready for her. It would always be ready.

Lena moved to a new city, changed her name, and tried to forget. But every Valentine's Day, a card would arrive in the post, no return address, no signature. Inside, a single line: 'You would have loved it here. — M.' And she would remember the lodge, the rooms, the empty nameplate, and the husband who had never learned to give a good gift but had learned, with terrible perfection, how to keep the women who dared to leave him.

content.alt: A dark, empty bedroom with a floral dress laid on the bed and a single grey hair on the pillow
content.caption: The room that was waiting for Lena, still waiting.
